speaker: interviewer
text: The date is February 5, 1997, the survivor's name is, Greta Klingsberg Hofmeister, the interviewer is Mindy Kornberg, the city is in Jerusalem, in Israel, and the language will be in English. My name is Mindy Kornberg, and I'm interviewing Greta Klingsberg, formerly Hofmeister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello, my name is Greta Klingsberg, formerly Hofmeister. I was born in Vienna, 11 September, '29. And I had, what I suppose you might call, a normal childhood. We were poor but very clean, and very proper, and very interested in children should learn and go to school, et cetera, et cetera. I had a sister, her name was [NON-ENGLISH] Hofmeister, unfortunately she did not survive. And my parents, my mother was Paula, formerly [? Steiner, ?] my father, Alfred Hofmeister. We were, of course, Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was-- different jobs. The last that I remember, he was working at some newspaper. I don't know in what capacity, but I know that he sort-- as a socialist, or something. That's one of the reasons which we come into later, why we went separately across the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, did she have an occupation outside the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother was at home. She-- before she married she was working as the first and only Jewess that-- what you might call a drugstore, or something, that sold newspapers, and cigars, and cigarettes. And I have even a picture with-- she was the only Jew. Because her parents died very young, and she had to support her younger brother. But since her marriage she stayed at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember extended family? Were there grandparents, and aunts, and uncles around, that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It's very hard to reconstruct it be-- yes, basically, my mother had a younger brother and two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give me their names, if you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, her brother is Leo [? Steiner, ?] her sisters were, Sophie and Anna, and-- that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did these people survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: The brother survived because he was married to a Christian lady, who is still alive, by the way, at the age of 89.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Katya-- [INAUDIBLE] [? Steiner. ?] I don't know her first na-- I mean, former name. [LAUGHS] And the sister-- another sister survived, Sophie, survived because she went to Italy, and later to America. Anna did not survive, neither did her son, Paul, and they perished in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was all your mother's side of the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on my mother's side. From my father, I remember only his mother, which was the second wife, I think, of his father. Because his father, my grandfather, I never knew. I only knew about him because he was like a [INAUDIBLE] in Berlin, of all the places.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leopold Hofmeister, but he went under the name of Paulie [? Hoffer. ?] And he always married the first ladies, so later on in Terezin, I met about three-- [LAUGHS] three females who were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Former grandmothers-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --grandmothers, but-- now my only grandmother died, as well. But the name Hofmeister was not so common, so whenever it popped up you knew it was some connected to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the neighborhood that you lived in? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in Vienna, of course. In Vienna we lived in-- we lived in the second district, which was called, the Mazzesinsel, like the island of Matzo, [LAUGHS] because it was very Jewish. And there was a big great synagogue. And I remember only an uncle, [INAUDIBLE], who did those little beautiful hand-scissored flags, or something, for the [NON-ENGLISH]. And I remember the synagogue, but we were not specially religious, but we were very Jewish. School was very nice, as long as I went there. I went like for two classes. I didn't even finish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a public school, not a Jewish school-- public school, but I was a very-- very interested in learning. It was a great thing for me to do. And I was very heartbroken when, in the second class, I didn't get all those good grades because, as they said, Hitler was already there, and Jewish kids are not supposed to be good pupils.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your first memories of that type of thing? Of like the beginnings of anti-Semitism there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't so [INAUDIBLE] because I could not comprehend it. It was very hard to explain to a seven-, eight-year-old child. So I don't remember much because we were very sheltered in this little-- poor, but very lovable family that she had. And that's all I remember. My first memory, actually, goes much later-- a little bit later, actually-- still in '38. But when we ran away from Austria to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any memories of friends in school when you were still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. It probably-- if I still had, it was wiped out by later, stronger memories that I still keep.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember from that period what were your ideas about the future for yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, of course, I wanted to be either an actress, or a teacher. A teacher to give something to the people, or an actress to make them laugh. I always found it very good to [INAUDIBLE]. And actually, in some capacity, I still stayed that way, because I was interested also in music. I guess, I was gifted. I didn't study any instrument and it was because we were quite poor. But that's all I, more or less, remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any other outside of school activities, things you liked to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that early time, no. I cannot say that I remember, even if my mother told me some things, I don't remember it. It only-- it was a retold memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what's the turning point for you, that's 1938?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we-- it was the first time that I had to take over, to be grown up, or to take responsibility for my mother, and for my smaller sister. My sister was, by the way, younger, a year younger than I. And when we crossed the bord-- I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you go back a little? How did that all begin? Like, when you remember first hearing that you have to leave, that the family had to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't we heard it. We just packed a few--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, from when you-- from your parents, you must have heard--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was not at home. I don't know why. Later on I was told that he was looked for by the Germans so he disappeared, or went underground. And we went separately, so that-- I can remember only that we were with my mother and not with my parents. And it was very cold, and very windy, so it-- I don't know, and rainy. And we've just crossed, I mean-- I don't know how we came to the border, but you went on foot.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was kind of-- it was a sudden move, or you don't remember planning ahead or hearing plans?

speaker: interviewee
text: If it was planned, of course they wouldn't tell children because they might talk, so nobody talked about it. So just one evening we went out and didn't come back. We left everything behind. And we came to the border, and we had to cross a lot of fields, and it was rainy and muddy. And I remember this squishy feeling of lifting your feet. I still can feel it in my body how heavy it was, because it was so full of mud, and whatever. And my mother was so exhausted with those two kids, she said, no, I've had enough, we are going back. And I started to stomp my foot, which I couldn't very well in this mud, and said, no, we went so far, we are going on. We are not going back. I don't know why I did it, I have no idea. I only know that I did, and that my mother said, yes, that's what you did. And from that moment, actually, I also was responsible already for my sister because half a year later, my parents, who were united in Brno, which, you know, it's the capital of Moravia, went on to Palestine. But this is a later story to come back. I know only how my mother was-- we didn't have any relatives on the border town of Czechoslovakia. It was Mikulov, or Nikolsburg. And my mother-- and we were standing there in the rain, and looking around, because we didn't know exactly whom to go to. But then we said, if somebody is going to talk German on the street, it must be Jews because it was a little Czech town [INAUDIBLE]. So and that was it. And they took us in, and fed us, and we could wash in warm water, [LAUGHS] and the next day we joined-- we went to Brno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the people's name by any chance?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was like [INAUDIBLE] years or more, years ago, I think. So, no, I don't remember. But I only remember how important it was that [LAUGHS] you found somebody who wouldn't give you to the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had that, as a child, you had that sense of danger, like you were escaping from something or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. I mean, we were already thrown out of school, they were already a few orders that we are not supposed to do, or yes, to do, or we could do. Definitely--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's why I asked that. Did you understand at the time what it was about? What was the feeling you remember having? Why this was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it went so fast, it went so fast. No, I did not. I only-- as the Germans were already in Vienna, it was-- you were not supposed to go on the sidewalk. It was all those little things starting, making you feel humiliated. But, as a child, I was 7, or 8, you don't get it, you have your own life, somehow, and you are protected by grown ups. But it was-- later you reconstruct, but I don't want to say about this, because I want to [INAUDIBLE]. You wanted me to tell about what I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's very [LAUGHS] hard to differentiate what you [INAUDIBLE]. So let's take it step by step. Yeah, so that was Vienna, and then later in Brno, where we joined my mother's-- my uncle, my mother's younger brother, who was married to a Christian lady, so they found already a place there. And we stayed together until my parents left. And they were not the only parents, there were 30 parents like these, whose children were German-speaking, coming from Austria, and we-- all of a sudden we were in Brno, in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had youth certificates for the children, put us into a children's home, not yet an orphanage, and went on an illegal transport to Palestine. It was too dangerous for children to take along. Nobody could predict what is more dangerous then. That it was impossible to take children on a journey like this. So we stayed behind. Meanwhile, unfortunately, Hitler came to Czechoslovakia. And so we were thrown out of this children's home. We were 30 children-- I only can tell you now, from this 30 children, only three survived, and without their brothers or sisters. So it's only Jackie, Alex, and me. So that's probably the percentage, anyway, of all these happenings then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran the children's home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, but only-- I know it was immediately closed the moment the Germans came, or after a month or two, and we were thrown into a Jewish orphanage. And this was quite horrid because, first of all, the orphans knew that we are children with parents, so that's already nothing in common. Then we did not have the language, we did not speak Czech, of course, we spoke German. So this was also not very agreeable. And it was quite a hard time to overcome these obstacles. We were thrown also out of the school, and so-- anyway, one way to learn another language, that's-- I acquired the Czech language, which I spoke until '46. I did not utter a word in German. Of course, I am fluent in German, because I was always read it. And my first-- talking German again was when I came to Palestine because my parents, unfortunately, did not have any other language. So when I finally met them-- and I did, which was lucky, I had to speak German, again.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stopped speaking German by choice--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --because you felt it made you an outsider at that point or just?

words.text: outsider at that point or just?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Not an outsider, because these were my enemies, so, I was not against the language. But also in Terezin I was only with Czech children, so I spoke Czech, and I read Czech, and we played Czech, and that's it. But I didn't-- somehow I was much more clever than now, I guess, because [LAUGHS] I didn't want to forget the language. And I always read, I always read.

speaker: interviewer
text: In German?

speaker: interviewee
text: In German, as well, yes. So that's how I didn't forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from '38-- end of '38, until '42, when we went to the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me a little bit of what it was like? I mean, that's quite a few years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of [LAUGHS] the Jewish version of? Horrible, really horrible. Because it was-- we were all in one room. I mean, all the boys in one room, all the girls in one room. It was from the age of five till 18. It was very cramped, it was-- no privacy, of course, no-- there was nothing enjoyable there. And the leading lady, Ms. Green, she was called, was really quite a sadist. I mean, it was very, a very horrible time, and only later, when we already spoke the language, which goes very fast with children, we united against her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had something in common. But it will sound strange, but the first time I felt again relaxed or happy as a child, was in Terezin, and not in the Jewish orphanage of Brno. Because in Terezin-- it's also the external things, of course, but in Terezin, we were all in the same boat. We were all Jewish, there was no problem anymore of being an orphan, or not knowing the language, or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all thrown together, and we knew we had to stick together, somehow. But going back to the orphanage, it was already more and more those anti-Jewish laws that came into existence in Czechoslovakia. We are not supposed to learn, we are not supposed to go and buy anything, and we couldn't buy anything. On top of that we were really hungry in this orphanage. It sounds so strange because it was no reason. And one of the reasons we went [INAUDIBLE] the first to Terezin was because this lady, Green, or whatever her name was, did some blackmail with the stuff that she got for the children, rationings of food. And so, of course, the German logic was, throw the whole orphanage into the camps. So we then went to Terezin when the Czech population was still there. It's also not so agreeable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the orphanage, what was the day like? What did you do, or not do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot remember, but we were punished for the silliest actions. We had to kneel down and stay the night kneeling and all these-- it sounds incredible, really incredible. So that's why, really, I almost wiped out of my memory. I only remember it was very bad, and I was really relieved when we came into the children's home of Terezin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you see, it's all a matter of-- a child has its own world, and the world to be deprived of parents, to be deprived of the language that you are fluent in, that was your own, and in this whole strange world, and every day another law, anti-you-- not you personally, but-- so it was really a horrible time. I thing the whole thing of the Shoah, of the Holocaust, is, until it comes to the camps it's not much-- it should be much more gone [INAUDIBLE] because this is really the deprivation of all your rights as a human being, or the differentiation between your neighbor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it develops the character of the neighbor, if you want it or not. All of a sudden you are not allowed to say, hello, or you are not-- and all these things, which I know now, of course, I was a child and didn't know, but I think this is even worse then the camps themselves. You find a modus vivendus afterwards. But it takes some time. But this here, you didn't know what to expect the next day, or the next second. And you were thrown into this goyish world, all of a sudden. [LAUGHS] They were not your friends anymore, not your neighbors, they were scared and this is quite--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was within the orphanages, or around the area you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around the area, I mean, generally speaking about the whole-- before you come into the camp, before you were thrown together with your own-- it was quite hard, I think, for the grownups, even more. Not to be able to drive, not to be able to go into the cinema, not to go into-- on a tram, not take a bus, not to go to a shop to buy anything, and not to sit on a park bench, and all these things. But somehow--

speaker: interviewer
text: During the period in the orphanage, you were together with your sister at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I was together, not only with my sister, with those 30 children, as well, who were like from our background. But, yes, yes. I was with my sister until a half year before the end of the war. All the time, together, even if we didn't sleep in the same room. In Terezin it was according to the age you were born-- I mean, to the year you were born-- so after working, you could, somehow, study something, or do something together. So we tried to-- I don't know whether you remember-- know about Terezin but it was like a Jewish serf government, whatever. And the children that did not have parents were put together somewhere into children's, what they call children's homes. And even parents put their children there, because it was better for children to be amongst children, then to see ever seeing in the camps quarters of the grownups. So we were together with children who were born in '29, for instance. And my sister was born in '30, so not a big difference, but it was a difference at that time. So she was in a different room, but in the same home, in the same house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's-- from the time you left the orphanage, could you tell me about that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I left the orphanage only to the transport to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, so tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the transport anymore, really. I only remember that we tried a few times to still reach Palestine. And this was really terrible because we were already once-- doesn't belonged to the Holocaust, but it is a Holocaust, we were already in the train and thrown out because Hitler had some meeting with Mussolini, or something--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? What year, if you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I don't know, this I don't-- I really don't know. But you can read it up somewhere. [LAUGHS] Anyway, and we were thrown back because there was a meeting of--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you say, we, who-- you and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, the children, the whole--

speaker: interviewer
text: All the children from the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not all the orphan-- all the children that had parents in Palestine were left behind-- with certificate to go to Palestine, legally. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was an attempt to take--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, an attempt to take them via another time, another time, yes. And my parents were already [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any communication from your parents all those years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Until the war broke out there was a possibility of corresponding, and also, I think, through the Red Cross, once or twice. And I only remember that I wrote to my parents, with this Red Cross letter, which I can't, at the moment, find, that they should do something really fast because, otherwise, we won't meet. So you see, as a child you are so clever. And unfortunately it was told they couldn't do anything, of course. They were without anything, they-- all they had was a rucksack, and a few books, and, as you saw already, my little Teddy bear, which is not so little, which I found after nine years, or eight years, here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe we could show it. Could you-- [INAUDIBLE] pass it over. It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Hello. This is my Teddy bear. [LAUGHS] Anyway, that's the oldest thing left of my early, early, childhood, which I don't even know. He doesn't have a name, but he is-- like he was sitting there, in our little apartment in Vienna, and I found him again, in '46, when I came to Palestine. And I was very happy. And he used to have a voice, but he lost it. No, you see. You can still hear if you want to. OK, that's so much for my poor parents. It's nothing. That's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you received letters back at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, but I guess I did, because there were some-- it was possible. I don't remember this. [BACKGROUND NOISE] It was impossible from Terezin to write, but I don't know whether anybody ever received it. And it was also impossible to get packages, care packages, to Terezin, which we never saw. And my parents, who didn't have anything, I mean, they came without anything to this country, to Israel-- then Palestine-- and they used to send parcels. But I never saw a parcel in the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you entered Terezin when?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning of '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about that, first arriving there, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I only remember-- that is one of the strongest memories of mine, is this, being crowded all the time, being crowded. Never having the possibility to be by yourself a little bit. And I was always very shy, and tried to read a book, or something, but it was impossible, you know. I remember-- this goes back, again, to the orphanage-- summertime we were sent always-- always-- I mean three, four, years we were there-- to Czech villages, to Jewish families who were in a better position, materially, and they took in orphans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So whenever they were looking for me, I was in-- also I was scared sick to-- of any cow, or anything bigger than a little dog. We were there, somehow, I was in the stable, with a book, finally being a little bit by myself. I needed this. It was [INAUDIBLE], but it was-- so that my memory of camps is only very, very crowded, of course no privacy at all. But the crowdedness of it, never being able to go, to walk, to just-- to have space. So this is one of the strongest-- and I still need space, and calm, and quietness. Also I love people, and I have a lot of friends, but it is very important to be by myself. You mention books a lot. You were a big reader, you liked to read. Do you remember any specific books that, maybe, helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not form the orphanage, I don't remember anything. This was normal-- normal, as yet. But in Terezin, I only remember that we read of course in Czech, and it was called [SPEAKING CZECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: About Van Gogh.

speaker: interviewee
text: About Van Gogh. And I still love Van Gogh. And that's how I came to meet him. And then, of all the things, By by Daphne du Maurier. I don't know why, because one book went from one hand to the other, but we read all the time. Because we could still-- to Terezin you could bring in, I think, about 20 kilos, which is about 40 pounds. And, of course, what do Jews bring? Books, at least then.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, again, let me ask you about, first, your first entrance to Terezin, what you remember about arriving, or the beginning of being there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The arriving-- I mean, the journey always is terrible, always very crowded, very cold, very noisy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a train or--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was walking and train, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the journey? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. This you have to ask somebody who is even older than I am because at the time I was 12 years old, so I don't remember, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sticking together with all the children, and of course, with my sister, who was my responsibility. So that I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your first living quarters like when you first--

speaker: interviewee
text: The first living quarters were still not organized because, as I told you, the Czech population was still there. Because we were like punished to go on with the first transports to Terezin. So, there was no condition, as yet. We were probably in one of those-- I don't know how you call this in English, it's like a cell, it's very-- were soldiers used to live. This was actually the garrison--

speaker: interviewer
text: The barracks-- the garrison--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not the barracks, it's-- yeah-- and that was-- actually, the whole of Terezin is a garrison. So they would squeeze in as much, as many people as possible. And it was possible, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, as far as food, or clothing, what you had? At the beginning, at least.

speaker: interviewee
text: Clothing, we had our own, what we bought. And the food was rationed, but it was there. And, as I told you, we already were quite hungry in the orphanage, so it was nothing new. So, I didn't suffer so much. Later on, I even earned my first food, I mean, like I told you-- I don't know. Anyway, by singing, [LAUGHS] by being chosen to sing there in a few choirs. So I earned my margarine and sugar. So that was my first professional entrance into the world of music, and earning my stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you first came there, were you given a job or--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, no, we were-- it was still a very chaotic-- the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Disorganized.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But later on, yes, we were-- then when we were already in the children's home, we went together to work, and together, after work, we learned together, or two of the children took the food, so we had it together. And it was somehow-- this organization was also-- finally came a lady-- whom was-- so the children, maybe, I don't know how many. But about-- so the children are the same age, more or less. And one grown up person, who was maybe 25, but for us, of course, very grown up already, was responsible for the children, and for the organization in that home-- cleaningness--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning it was Magda, she went on to the east, which we later knew it was to [INAUDIBLE]. And then was Laura. And this is quite a story because I didn't want to see her, or talk to her at all, because she took the place of the first one, and I was loyal, still, to the first. And slowly-- I was at that moment, at that time, I was sick, I was in some quarantine-- separated. So I don't know. And she already won over the whole group of children. And in the end, of course, me, as well. Because I gave her my diary, and that was like the opening. And she took me-- actually, any child who did not have parents in the camps was assigned a lady, or au pair was assigned to take the children as their own. So this Laura [INAUDIBLE] was my foster mother. Luckily she saved me, my soul, my being, and also my physics, until I came to Palestine later. Because the whole time I was with her through all the camps. And after the war, as well, she made a place for me to stay with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we have to stop now for a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why I didn't--
